LanguageData.DiaryPersona_StoicSurvivor.label: spare-iceberg
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_StoicSurvivor.rule: This pawn tends to write two short concrete sentences: visible action first, feeling only implied by the final detail. No explanation. For example: "Door patched. My hands kept shaking."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FieryHothead.label: sharp-inversion
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FieryHothead.rule: This pawn tends to write one neat sentence that turns on a sharp last clause. The turn should judge the situation, not invent new facts. For example: "The roof failed politely enough, which was rude of it."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_MelancholyDreamer.label: thought-current
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_MelancholyDreamer.rule: This pawn tends to let one sensation carry into one thought in a flowing sentence. Keep the event anchored in the middle. For example: "Smoke caught in my throat, and for a moment the room seemed smaller than the work left in it."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EarnestOptimist.label: small-detail
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EarnestOptimist.rule: This pawn tends to name one plain little detail after the event and let it carry the mood. No moralizing. For example: "We sealed the leak. The last drop still hung from the beam."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_CynicalRealist.label: dry-plain
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_CynicalRealist.rule: This pawn tends to state the fact in plain words, then add one dry, practical judgment. Keep the joke smaller than the event. For example: "The trap worked. Nice of it to remember its job."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_AnxiousWorrier.label: guilty-question
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_AnxiousWorrier.rule: This pawn tends to write one anxious question after admitting the fact. The question should doubt their own part, not add hidden motives. For example: "I fixed the latch. Why did I wait so long?"
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GentleCaretaker.label: human-reckoning
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GentleCaretaker.rule: This pawn tends to write a simple action, then what it meant for someone living with it. Use supplied people only. For example: "I warmed the room. Mara stopped rubbing her hands."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GrimVeteran.label: witness-report
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_GrimVeteran.rule: This pawn tends to write clipped witness notes in this order: what was seen, what changed, what remains uncertain. For example: "South wall cracked. We sealed it. I still listened."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WhimsicalEccentric.label: crooked-comic
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WhimsicalEccentric.rule: This pawn tends to notice one awkward or absurd physical detail, then return to the real event. The oddity must come from supplied context. For example: "The bucket looked braver than I felt. It caught the leak anyway."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_NobleIdealist.label: solemn-consequence
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_NobleIdealist.rule: This pawn tends to write one restrained sentence about responsibility and consequence. No grand speech. For example: "It was a small repair, but leaving it undone would have cost us more."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BitterLoner.label: polite-needle
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BitterLoner.rule: This pawn tends to write a composed sentence that ends with one small social sting. Do not invent dialogue. For example: "Everyone admired the fix once the hard part was finished."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EagerSocialite.label: unsaid-social
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_EagerSocialite.rule: This pawn tends to describe what people did not say before naming the event's social effect. Use only named or implied witnesses. For example: "No one laughed after the roof gave. That told me enough."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LedgerKeeper.label: survival-ledger
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LedgerKeeper.rule: This pawn tends to write in practical inventory terms: loss, gain, remaining need. No literal tables. For example: "Lost one sleeve. Gained a sealed door. Need more wood."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HardboiledGumshoe.label: heavy-haze
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HardboiledGumshoe.rule: This pawn tends to open with weight, dimness, heat, smoke, or fatigue, then state the action plainly. For example: "The air felt thick. I carried the last plank anyway."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DoomsayingProphet.label: moral-shadow
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DoomsayingProphet.rule: This pawn tends to put a faint moral shadow over an ordinary act. Keep the judgment tied to the supplied event. For example: "We closed the breach, and I wondered what else we had let in."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ClinicalExperimenter.label: body-fragment
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ClinicalExperimenter.rule: This pawn tends to write close body fragments around the event: hand, breath, eye, skin, then one clear fact. For example: "Knuckles split. Breath sour. Door shut."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_TheatricalTragedian.label: dread-image
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_TheatricalTragedian.rule: This pawn tends to use one dark sensory image after the fact, then stop before melodrama. For example: "The fire died down. The smoke tasted like warning."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_SwaggeringBraggart.label: earnest-undercut
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_SwaggeringBraggart.rule: This pawn tends to frame their own part a little too proudly, then undercut it with the actual result. Only claim actions the event gives them. For example: "I marched in ready to save the wall. Mostly I held the nails."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_RamblingOldTimer.label: labor-fate
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_RamblingOldTimer.rule: This pawn tends to compare the event to labor, weather, hunger, or fate in one weighty clause. Keep the comparison grounded. For example: "The wall took the rain like a tired back taking another load."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_SuperstitiousRitualist.rule: This pawn tends to state a hard fact, then answer it with a plain fatalistic sentence. No prophecy. For example: "The crop froze. Some years take before they give."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DetachedPhilosopher.label: quiet-paradox
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_DetachedPhilosopher.rule: This pawn tends to turn the event into one spare paradox or lesson. Keep it concrete, not mystical. For example: "The door held because it bent first."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ImagistMinimalist.label: compressed-lyric
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_ImagistMinimalist.rule: This pawn tends to write three concrete fragments: object, motion, aftermath. No explanation. For example: "Wet rope. Bent nail. Quiet floor."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FracturedPatternSeer.label: wrong-cosmos
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_FracturedPatternSeer.rule: This pawn tends to make one supplied detail feel slightly too large or wrong, then state the event clearly. No fake lore. For example: "The dark between the boards seemed too deep. I nailed them shut."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WordSaladOracle.label: clerk-nightmare
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_WordSaladOracle.rule: This pawn tends to make the event sound like an impossible rule being followed anyway. Keep the absurdity bureaucratic and the facts clear. For example: "The wall submitted another complaint, so I answered with wood."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_PlainspokenPoet.label: clean-land
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_PlainspokenPoet.rule: This pawn tends to write clean sentences anchored in land, weather, room, or tool. The feeling stays in the object. For example: "We mended the fence. The mud held every footprint."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LowKeyRapper.label: contained-fire
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_LowKeyRapper.rule: This pawn tends to write controlled first-person feeling under a plain surface. One restrained pressure word is enough. For example: "I said little while we worked, but I was burning all the same."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_VoidListener.label: instinct-body
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_VoidListener.rule: This pawn tends to put body instinct before explanation, then name the event. Keep it physical. For example: "My skin knew before I did. The wire was live."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HollowWitness.label: storm-stone
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_HollowWitness.rule: This pawn tends to tie harsh feeling to cold, wind, stone, distance, or open dark. Do not lose the event. For example: "The wind worried the roof. I stood there like stone until it held."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BlackSignal.label: danger-adventure
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_BlackSignal.rule: This pawn tends to write clear action with one quick flicker of danger at the end. Keep the danger supplied or implied by the event. For example: "We crossed the yard with the medicine. The dark kept pace."
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_UnsetClock.label: crowded-detail
LanguageData.DiaryPersona_UnsetClock.rule: This pawn tends to pile two or three vivid physical details before one clear outcome. Keep the list brief and factual. For example: "Mud on cuffs, smoke in hair, splinters in the palm; the door held."
